content.problemStatement:
Implement the Randomised quick sort algorithm. The steps of the algorithm are as follows:


Choose a random element from the array as the pivot.
Partition the array into two sub-arrays: elements less than the pivot and elements greater than the pivot.
Recursively apply the Randomised Quick Sort algorithm to the sub-arrays.
Combine the sorted sub-arrays along with the pivot to obtain the sorted array.
The code given in the template is an implementation of the Quick sort algorithm and will give you a TLE. 

Try to Implement the algorithm given above to solve this problem.


Input Format

The first line of the input contains a single integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The description of T test cases follows.
The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN - the number of integers of the array
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains an integer N, the size of the array, and the second line contains N space‑separated integers.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output the N integers of the sorted array in non‑decreasing order on a single line, separated by a single space.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Both arrays are sorted into non‑decreasing order using Randomised Quick Sort.
The descending array becomes sorted after applying the algorithm.
A single element array is already sorted.

solution.approach: We apply the classic quick‑sort algorithm but choose the pivot uniformly at random in every recursive call. Randomising the pivot destroys the worst‑case O(N^2) behaviour that occurs for already sorted or adversarial inputs, giving an expected running time of O(N log N). The algorithm works as follows: 1) Pick a random index between low and high, swap that element with the last element (the pivot). 2) Partition the sub‑array so that all elements smaller than the pivot are placed before it and the rest after it. 3) Recursively sort the left and right parts. Because the pivot is random, the depth of recursion is O(log N) on average, so the total expected time per test case is O(N log N) and the extra space is only the recursion stack.
solution.timeComplexity: Expected O(N log N) per test case (overall O(T·N log N)).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(log N) auxiliary stack space on average (in‑place sorting, no extra arrays).

code.Python3:
import sys,random
sys.setrecursionlimit(1000000)

def partition(a,low,high):
    pivot_idx = random.randint(low,high)
    a[pivot_idx],a[high]=a[high],a[pivot_idx]
    pivot=a[high]
    i=low
    for j in range(low,high):
        if a[j] < pivot:
            a[i],a[j]=a[j],a[i]
            i+=1
    a[i],a[high]=a[high],a[i]
    return i

def quick_sort(a,low,high):
    if low < high:
        p=partition(a,low,high)
        quick_sort(a,low,p-1)
        quick_sort(a,p+1,high)

def main():
    data=sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it=iter(data)
    T=int(next(it))
    out_lines=[]
    for _ in range(T):
        N=int(next(it))
        arr=[int(next(it)) for _ in range(N)]
        quick_sort(arr,0,N-1)
        out_lines.append(' '.join(map(str,arr)))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out_lines))

if __name__=='__main__':
    main()
